title: Quantum Processing
short: Photonic approaches to quantum information processing based on ultrafast time-bin encoding, programmable interferometry, nonclassical light, and measurement.
body: We develop photonic tools for quantum processing in regimes where light can be prepared, transformed, and measured with high temporal resolution. This includes ultrafast time-bin encoding, programmable temporal interferometry, photon-number-resolved measurements, homodyne-style readout, and nonlinear optical operations.

title: Quantum Communications and Networking
short: Protocols and devices for quantum communication, including high-dimensional encoding, quantum key distribution, switching, and network-compatible photonics.
body: We study quantum communication and networking using optical systems that are compatible with deployed photonic infrastructure. Topics include quantum key distribution, high-dimensional encodings, telecom-band photon manipulation, optical switching, and interfaces for future quantum networks.

title: Quantum Memory
short: Light–matter systems and ultrafast control methods for storing, retrieving, and manipulating optical quantum states.
body: We investigate approaches for coupling photonic quantum states to material systems. This includes ultrafast light–matter interactions, Raman processes, coherent optical control, and memory-compatible sources and measurements.

title: Quantum Imaging
short: Imaging methods that use correlations, single-photon detection, and quantum-inspired measurement to extract spatial or temporal information.
body: We develop imaging techniques that exploit optical correlations and single-photon detection. Research directions include quantum and classical intensity interferometry, ghost imaging, holography, and imaging approaches that connect spatial, temporal, and spectral degrees of freedom.

title: Quantum Sensing
short: Quantum and photonic measurement strategies for sensing, metrology, beam tracking, and weak-signal detection.
body: We explore how quantum states of light, structured modes, and high-efficiency detection can improve measurement. This includes quantum-enhanced beam tracking, metrology with structured photons, low-light detection, and sensing protocols based on correlations and nonclassical statistics.
